did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model to classify skin cancer lesions using the Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000 dataset. The CNN was trained and validated to achieve a precision accuracy score of 98.88% for automated diagnosis of seven different categories of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design, training, and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (98.88% accuracy) and includes sections on methods, results, and discussion of new findings, classifying it as a research paper despite being published in a conference proceedings.

title: Towards Dermatoscopic Imaging and CNN Based Accurate Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Control, Computing and Communications (IC3)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of accurate skin cancer detection by designing an automated diagnostic system using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The model was trained and validated on the Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven lesion categories. Data augmentation techniques were applied to the 80% training subset to handle class imbalance and improve generalization, with the remaining 20% reserved for validation. The proposed CNN model achieved a precision accuracy score of 98.88%, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods such as ELM and VGG-16. The results demonstrate the model's high effectiveness in classifying various skin lesions, highlighting its potential as a robust diagnostic tool for early intervention.

all_task_types.target: Seven different categories of skin lesions (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, VASC, and MEL)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task using a CNN model to diagnose and categorize skin cancer lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for seven classes. Performance is reported using accuracy (98.88%), precision, recall, and F1-score for each class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermatoscopic images (DIs) collected from Kaggle, containing high-resolution images representing seven classes of skin lesions.

use.training: 80% of the dataset was allocated for training the CNN model.
use.validation: The remaining 20% of the dataset was used for validation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, precision, recall, and F1-score.

Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000.notes: The proposed CNN model achieved an overall accuracy of 98.88%, surpassing existing state-of-the-art methods including ELM, VGG-16, InvolutionNet, and others. Misclassifications were minimal, primarily occurring in the BKL class (misclassified as AKIEC and NV) and between BKL/AKIEC or MEL/DF due to class overlap.

architecture_used.pretraining: None reported; the paper states future work will incorporate Transfer Learning (TL), but the current model was trained from scratch on the HAM10000 dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: 28x28x3 (28x28 pixel images with 3 RGB color channels)

layers: Multiple Convolutional (Conv.) layers with varying filter sizes (e.g., 3x3 and 7x7)
Batch Normalization layers (applied after each Conv. layer)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotating, flipping, zooming, and cropping applied to the training dataset to increase variety and prevent overfitting.
Input resizing: Images resized to 28x28 pixels with 3 RGB color channels.
Batch normalization: Applied after each convolutional layer to normalize activations.
Max pooling: 2x2 filter used to lower spatial dimensions of feature maps.

conclusions: The proposed custom CNN model achieved a precision accuracy score of 98.88% on the Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000 dataset, outperforming state-of-the-art methods including ELM, VGG-16, InvolutionNet, and others.
Data augmentation techniques (rotating, flipping, zooming, cropping) were effective in handling class imbalance and improving the model's generalization capabilities.
The model demonstrated high robustness and effective convergence, with training and validation accuracies converging close to 99% after 15 epochs.
The study acknowledges that variability in lesion appearance (due to skin tone, lighting, resolution) and dataset imbalance remain significant challenges in dermatological AI.
The interpretability of CNN models is identified as a critical limitation for clinical adoption, as they often function as black boxes lacking transparency in decision-making.
Future work will focus on incorporating additional data sources and transfer learning techniques to further enhance model robustness and diagnostic accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance (particularly underrepresentation of melanoma) and demographic bias (predominantly fair-skinned populations), limiting generalizability to diverse global populations.
The model was trained on a single 80/20 split without external validation or cross-validation, raising concerns about overfitting and the robustness of the reported 98.88% accuracy.
The CNN architecture uses a very small input resolution of 28x28 pixels, which may discard critical dermatoscopic details necessary for accurate diagnosis of subtle lesion features.

The paper acknowledges that CNNs function as 'black boxes' with poor interpretability, a significant barrier to clinical adoption where explainability is crucial for building trust with medical practitioners.

Performance metrics show high variance across classes; while some classes (AKIEC, DF, VASC) achieved perfect scores, others (NV, BKL) had lower recall and F1-scores, indicating potential failure modes in specific diagnostic scenarios.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical reliability of the results compared to baselines.

Data augmentation techniques (rotating, flipping, zooming, cropping) were applied only to the training set, which is standard practice, but the paper does not discuss potential artifacts introduced by these augmentations in dermatoscopic imaging.
